item.title: Santa Maria della Salute
item.museum: The Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York
item.lesson: A generation after Canaletto, Francesco Guardi painted the same city and told a different truth. His subject here is the church every Venice painter must eventually face: Santa Maria della Salute, the great baroque octagon raised in thanksgiving after the plague of 1630, its dome ringed with stone scrolls like breaking waves. But look at what Guardi's brush does to all that marble. Edges flicker. Figures become quick dark dashes he called macchiette, little stains. The water has stopped being a pavement and started being weather. Venice's power was fading in Guardi's lifetime — the Republic had thirty years left — and his nervous, sparkling touch reads now like a city seen through its own mortality, beautiful the way late afternoon is beautiful. Canaletto counted what light touched. Guardi noticed that light itself trembles. That small crack in the veduta's certainty is the opening every painter after him will walk through.
item.lookFor: Compare the dome's crisp silhouette against the sky with its wobbling reflection below — Guardi lets the water disagree with the architecture, and believes the water.
